coredata.dc:title: Parametric study of battery module cooling: Configuration optimization using response curve method
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Ghimire, Daya Bandhu
Maharjan, Sanjeev
Neupane, Saroj
Uprety, Abhishek
coredata.dc:description: Recently, cylindrical lithium-ion battery cells have been widely used in electric vehicles due to efficient automated production and cheap kilowatt-hour capacity. For optimal operation of a cell, the internal temperature of the cell should be maintained within the range of 15–35 °C by employing suitable cooling mechanism. This study comprehensively compares multiple air-cooling configurations specifically designed for lithium-ion battery three-series and three-parallel (3S3P) modules using. The methodology involves an experimental phase to measure the parameters of Bernardi's equation, essential for calculating heat generation with consideration of both Joule's heat and entropic heat. Subsequently, a numerical simulation using Ansys Fluent examines battery heat generation within the 3S3P modules, employing diverse air-cooling configurations. Each configuration undergoes simulation with the aid of design of experiments and parametric analysis, allowing the observation of cooling behavior across various input parameters. To ensure reliability of simulation, the cooling of the unit cell module is simulated under similar conditions. The comparison between these simulated results and experimental values is conducted to validate the accuracy of the overall analysis methodology. The heat measurements indicated a unit cell heat output of 0.9028 W at an average rate of 0.85C. Among the investigated air-cooling configurations, the SIDO (Single Input Double Output) arrangement stood out as the most effective in maintaining a consistently lower average cell temperature, as determined through the application of the response curve method. This study emphasizes the importance of air-cooling design for improving the performance and safety of lithium-ion battery modules used in electric vehicles.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion cell
Parametric study
Battery module cooling
3S3P module
Response curve method
Configuration optimization

Experimental setup for unit cell module air cooling.
CAD model of unit cell experiment setup.
Air cooling configurations of (a) Z shaped geometry,(b) SISO geometry, (c) U shaped geometry and (d) SIDO geometry.
Average temperature vs time graph for module cooling at various speeds.
Response curve of temperature vs velocity.
Temperature countour of (a) Z shaped geometry,(b) U shaped geometry, (c) SISO geometry and (d) SIDO geometry.
Average temperature vs velocity for different geometries.
Material properties of materials used in module development.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: In accordance with the experimental setup outlined in section 2.1.2, several parameters were measured to assess entropic heat. The collected data, including temperatures and open circuit voltage (OCV), is cataloged and presented in Table 3 below.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Simulation result of cell average temperature.
Comparison of experimental value vs response curve value.

head.ce:title: Parametric study of battery module cooling: Configuration optimization using response curve method

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering, Pulchowk Campus, Institute of Engineering, Lalitpur, Nepal

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering, Pulchowk Campus, Institute of Engineering, Lalitpur, Nepal

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, cylindrical lithium-ion battery cells have been widely used in electric vehicles due to efficient automated production and cheap kilowatt-hour capacity. For optimal operation of a cell, the internal temperature of the cell should be maintained within the range of 15–35 °C by employing suitable cooling mechanism. This study comprehensively compares multiple air-cooling configurations specifically designed for lithium-ion battery three-series and three-parallel (3S3P) modules using. The methodology involves an experimental phase to measure the parameters of Bernardi's equation, essential for calculating heat generation with consideration of both Joule's heat and entropic heat. Subsequently, a numerical simulation using Ansys Fluent examines battery heat generation within the 3S3P modules, employing diverse air-cooling configurations. Each configuration undergoes simulation with the aid of design of experiments and parametric analysis, allowing the observation of cooling behavior across various input parameters. To ensure reliability of simulation, the cooling of the unit cell module is simulated under similar conditions. The comparison between these simulated results and experimental values is conducted to validate the accuracy of the overall analysis methodology. The heat measurements indicated a unit cell heat output of 0.9028 W at an average rate of 0.85C. Among the investigated air-cooling configurations, the SIDO (Single Input Double Output) arrangement stood out as the most effective in maintaining a consistently lower average cell temperature, as determined through the application of the response curve method. This study emphasizes the importance of air-cooling design for improving the performance and safety of lithium-ion battery modules used in electric vehicles.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The implications of climate change have driven the development of batteries to reduce carbon footprint and promote the adoption of sustainable energy sources [1]. As a consequence, the transition from internal combustion engines to electric vehicles can be seen as a significant step toward electric vehicle technology [2]. Moreover, the number of electric cars on the roads globally went up from about 1 million in 2014 to around 7.2 million in 2019. Experts predict a massive jump to 116 million electric cars by 2030 [3]. Lithium-ion batteries have become the leading choice in the electric car industry's energy storage market due to various advantages, including a high energy density relative to their weight, substantial power storage capacity, a prolonged lifespan, minimal self-discharge, compact dimensions, and low self-discharge rate.

Battery consists of a number of cells in which there is structured anode and cathode that contain lithium [1]. There are many types of cells on basis of their geometry like prismatic, pouch, and cylindrical cells. Cylindrical cells are the most used due to the high degree of standardization from a manufacturing perspective. In addition, the cylindrical cell has better behavior in terms of heat dissipation because of the sound heat dissipation space which is formed between the cylinders while packing [4]. To achieve the required voltage and storage capacity, a battery module is developed by joining together several individual cylindrical cells. They are arranged in certain series and parallel combination according to current and voltage requirement to power the system through discharging action.

While batteries are being discharged or charged, they produce heat. Heat generation occurs during both charging and discharging process. Furthermore, heat generation in two electrodes, separator, current collectors, and tabs which are present inside cell contribute to total heat generation [5]. According to Peng et al. four types of heat are produced during operation of battery which are reaction heat, side reaction heat, joule heat, and polarization heat [4]. Joule's heating of the battery cell is linked with a change in potential drop in circuit which is directly proportional to charging rate. The other types of heat are combinedly termed as entropic heat. Entropic heat originates from reversible entropy change during the electro-chemical reaction due to intercalation and deintercalation of electrons [6]. Also, heat generation associated with battery is divided into reversible and irreversible heat [7]. The generation of reversible heat in cells occurs at cathode and anode. Battery heating can cause complicated problems to battery and vehicle. If this excessive heat generation is not controlled, the battery's lifetime and efficiency will eventually decline and in the worst-case, thermal runaway occurs and finally the battery module will explode. This thermal runaway in the lithium-ion battery occurs due to over-charging, internal short circuits, crushing or overheating. These might result in adverse situations like fire, smoke, gas emissions and decomposition of electrolyte and electrode material [11]. Excessive heat generation relates to an increase in battery temperature degrading the battery life. At every 15° increase in temperature there is decrease in 30 % of life cycle of cell [8]. On the other hand, the internal resistance of lithium-ion batteries increases significantly below 0 °C, which clearly reduces their capacity [9]. Researchers have investigated how the performance of lithium-ion batteries is affected by the temperature distribution inside the battery module. Their results showed that temperature inconsistencies could result in various charging and discharging properties, which would further lead to electrically imbalanced modules and a decline in battery module performance [10]. Maintaining temperature control during the operation of a battery is crucial [6]. It is essential to manage and dissipate this heat because elevated temperatures can have a direct impact on the safety of the battery [11]. The challenge arising in battery technology is thermal safety, significantly impacting its performance, safety, and efficiency [12]. The problems can be solved only through the development of effective battery thermal management system whose primary goal is to maintain the operational temperature within desired range [13]. The battery thermal management system is commonly categorized into two types: passive and active systems. Passive cooling methods, exemplified by phase change materials, operate without the need for external energy. In contrast, active cooling methods, which include fans, developed air-flowing channels, fin structures, and air cooling, are implemented to overcome the limitations of passive cooling.

Air cooling represents the most conventional method for battery thermal management systems, effectively maintaining the battery temperature within an acceptable range [14]. The systems are straightforward, cheap, electrically safe, small, leak-free, and require little maintenance. Natural cooling was found to be insufficient to fulfill the needs of an elevated temperature environment for larger battery module thermal management systems. Forced-air cooling is employed to circumvent these limits, along with the installation of fans, developed air-flowing channels, fin structures, and so on [15]. Forced convection improves the heat efficiency within a battery system as compared to natural convection due to its greater capacity for heat dissipation [16]. A variety of air-cooling techniques are particularly useful for cooling the battery module, but none of them take the battery module's ingress protection into consideration. In most cases, air is passed directly through cells for analysis and experimental validation [17]. A continuous flow of air is supplied from the outside of the driving electric vehicle at battery systems, dissipating heat created inside the module. A researcher examined the effect of cell spacing during forced convection in a 4*6 cylindrical battery module [18]. Battery module cooling efficiency was analyzed using a three-dimensional numerical model and an analytical thermal resistance model of a staggered battery module was constructed. It was found that the ideal cooling channel size for an 18,650 lithium-ion battery is 1 mm when considering the maximum temperature, space utilization, and energy efficiency factor [19]. In battery module designs, “the pin-fin channel design" and “the fin outside design" cooling configurations, the cell-level thermal and pressure drop performance of the air-cooling concept was studied using three-dimensional computational fluid dynamic models [20]. The structural optimization of the forced air-cooling system for lithium-ion battery heat dissipation was studied using parameters airflow channel with the air input angle, and the air-outlet angle. Zhang et al. investigated the performance of battery thermal management systems with various inlet and output positions [21]. The results showed that when cooling air entered the battery thermal management system from the bottom to the top, the temperature uniformity of the battery pack could be significantly improved.

As discussed above, this study analyses and compares different air-cooling configurations specifically designed for lithium-ion battery three-series and three-parallel (3S3P) parametrically using response curve method. An experimental procedure measures heat generation in a cell using Bernardi's equation. Ansys Fluent helps to design 3S3P modules for conducting numerical simulations of battery heat generation within the module, applying various air-cooling configurations. Parametric analysis, employing tools like design of experiments and the response curve method, generates extensive datasets for each configuration within Ansys Fluent. This facilitates the observation of cooling behavior across diverse input parameters for each configuration. Similarly, a unit cell module uses Ansys toolbox to simulate and observe the cell average temperature under the same input conditions and parameters. The results from simulation are compared against the experimental results of the cooling of the unit cell module to ensure reliability of simulation and hence to validate the analysis method. The main novelty of this work is the use of parametric analysis for cooling of battery module which has not been considered in the other research. Also, Ingress protection is considered in past literatures which will provide a more realistic analysis of battery modules, mirroring real-world conditions where such protections are inherent. This comparative study of various airflow configurations could be beneficial for the EV industry in selecting effective air-cooling configurations. Such choices can significantly impact battery longevity and enhance the safety of battery modules.

This section presents the theoretical framework and experimental setup for determining heat generation in a single cell, outlining the design of air-cooling configurations, providing context for the parametric analysis, and elucidating the unit cell analysis. As illustrated in Fig. 1 below, the methodology is initially validated through experimentation on the unit cell module. Subsequently, the focus shifts to the design and analysis of air-cooling configurations to identify the most optimal design.

Heat generation takes place within a cell during both charging and discharging processes. Evaluating heat generation in lithium-ion cells involves various methods such as calorimetry and electro-impedance spectroscopy, requiring setups that involve considerable costs. According to paper [9], on a cell, two types of heat generation occur. Firstly, Joule's heating takes place internally within the cell, stemming from the cell's inherent internal resistance. Secondly, entropic heat is produced due to the alteration in entropy during electrochemical reactions. The computation of different parameters relies on the formula provided below:
(1)
Internal
resistance
of
the
cell
( r )
=
O
c v
− V
I
(2)
Joule ’ s
Heat
(
Q j
)
= E × I −
I 2
× r
(3)
Entropy
Heat
(
Q r
)
= I × T ×
d V
d T
(4)
Total
heat
( Q )
=
Q j
+
Q r
= E × I −
(
I 2
)
× r + I × T ×
d V
d T
were,

O
c v
is open circuit voltage,

V
is loaded voltage,

I
is current flow through a circuit,
T
is an absolute temperature and

E
is the emf of cell.

The heat generation of a single cell was assessed using experimental setups, with a particular focus on the INR 18650 25S lithium-ion cell, which was selected for both heat measurement and subsequent module development.

The evaluation of irreversible heat entailed a methodical approach aimed at determining the internal resistance of a lithium-ion cell. This process is comprised of several critical stages. Initially, the battery underwent a complete discharge cycle to ensure stability. Afterward, the selection of an external resistance and multimeters equipped with battery testing capabilities was undertaken. The battery's positive and negative terminals were securely interfaced with the testing apparatus.

Subsequently, precise measurements of the open circuit voltage and resistance were obtained from the circuit shown in Fig. 2 above. With the application of a known current to the battery and the assessment of the resulting voltage across the cell, the use of Ohm's law facilitated the precise computation of the cell's internal resistance.

In Fig. 3 , the setup illustrates the utilization of an ammeter and voltmeter to measure the current passing through and the voltage across a resistor connected to a cylindrical cell. These measurements are subsequently employed to compute the internal resistance of the cell.

For entropic heat measurement, an assembled unit cell lithium-ion module with diverse components such as the charging module, temperature sensors are considered as shown in Fig. 4 . The module was strategically positioned within an airflow channel to observe change in voltage of cell along with change in temperature. Initially, experimental setup design was developed using parameters from battery cells’ specifications. Then, subsequent construction employed commonly available workshop tools or alternative manufacturing methods.

The fan module depicted in Fig. 4 was connected to the setup, but it remained inactive during observations as our objective was to record temperature and voltage with the fan turned off. All essential parameters were meticulously recorded for subsequent entropic heating calculations. This approach guaranteed the development of a well-structured and functional module that could be conveniently adapted for experimental purposes or real-world applications.

Analyzing the unit cell module was crucial step to validate the accuracy and reliability of the method and values used in numerical simulations. This analysis entailed validating against unit cell experimental results and observing any discrepancies to ensure precision. This process aided in validating the overall numerical analysis method, ultimately saving both time and costs associated with conducting module-level analyses of air-cooling configurations.

An experiment was conducted for unit module air cooling system to observe heat production of a lithium-ion cell responding to air cooling at varying air inlet velocities. The thermal behavior was affected by charging rate [22], but it was kept constant. The fan was operated at four distinct velocities, encompassing normal air velocity, specifically 0.2 m/s [23], within a standard room environment. It was assumed that utilizing two thermometers would suffice to estimate the cell's average temperature. Additionally, the cell's behavior during both discharge and charge cycles was presumed to be consistent. Air served as an approximation to fill the gap between the cell and aluminum, supplemented by polyamide tape, wood, paper, and thermal sensors. Based on these assumptions, the setup was developed for analysis as presented in Fig. 5 .

In the experiment, a wire was connected from the negative and positive terminals to the charging module. The temperature of the cell was indicated by the display of a thermocouple temperature controller. Nickel strips served as busbars in both terminals of the lithium-ion cells. These busbars were covered by Kapton tape, chosen for its effective electrical insulation properties. The cell underwent charging at varying current inputs, during which the current was monitored over time. Simultaneously, the average temperature was estimated using two temperature sensors positioned within the aluminum container housing the cell.

Numerical analysis was performed utilizing the Ansys battery model within Ansys Fluent. Ansys Design Modeler was employed to generate a CAD model for parametric numerical analysis. This software facilitated the creation of a comprehensive 3D model representing the experimental setup, encompassing cells and all associated components. Rigorous refinement and optimization of the model were undertaken to ensure precise replication of the physical characteristics inherent in the cell and experiment. Designing a CAD model for a battery cell experiment necessitated meticulous attention to detail regarding size, shape, and component intricacies.

The above CAD model in Fig. 6 was subjected to numerical analysis involving varied air velocities, obtained from the experimental setup at ambient temperature of 295 K. The system underwent direct simulation employing conjugate heat transfer analysis. Additionally, the design of experiments was utilized to generate a response curve, facilitating parametric analysis of the setup.

The numerical simulation employs conjugate heat transfer analysis, a method that addresses simultaneous heat exchange between solid and fluid domains. Conjugate heat transfer involves both convective and conductive heat exchange mechanisms. It delineates the fluid and solid as distinct domains, allowing for the examination and analysis of their interaction in the heat transfer process.

Initially, a Computer-Aided Design (CAD) model depicting the problem is created for direct simulation. Subsequently, the model undergoes meshing using suitable software, such as ANSYS Fluent. Upon completion of the meshing process, the file progresses to a processing stage where inputs, boundary conditions, and material properties are specified. The heat generation data derived from experimental results is utilized within the direct simulation. The analysis conducted was steady-state and involved a comprehensive three-dimensional (3D) analysis performed using Ansys Fluent. It is noteworthy that the ambient temperature significantly influences the temperature of the battery [24]. Consequently, to maintain consistency across all experiments, the ambient temperature is held constant which is the average value taken from experiment. The physical properties of materials serve as an essential input for the cell. While battery discharge inherently involves transient behavior, certain applications might benefit from a steady-state approach with high accuracy. For instance, when the thermal time constant is notably smaller than the discharge period, batteries tend to reach a quasi-stationary state. In such cases, the steady-state solution yields highly precise results. In thermal simulations, the energy and user scalar equations are exclusively solved. Conjugate heat transfer coupling has been employed in the analysis process. Subsequently, the steady-state solution is observed and subjected to post-processing to generate presentable results. The direct simulation phase is clearly delineated in the flowchart depicted in Fig. 7 below.

The parametric analysis approach was conducted utilizing the Ansys Design Exploration toolbox. The primary goal was to establish a clear correlation between input parameters and the resulting output parameter, minimizing the need for an exhaustive number of simulations that could be time-consuming. This systematic approach facilitated a comprehensive analysis while efficiently exploring the impact of various parameters on the output, streamlining the computational effort involved in evaluating multiple configurations. As illustrated in Fig. 7, the parametric analysis involves the utilization of Design of Experiments (DOE). DOE serves as a method to effectively sample a design space and construct a statistical model for predicting responses. In this specific scenario, after postprocessing of base case solution, the input and output parameters are assigned. The Design of Experiments (DOE) is executed employing sparse grid initialization to interpolate extensive datasets utilizing only three data points. From this dataset, a response curve is generated, offering a graphical representation of the data. This is achieved through refinement points that converge the error close to zero. Utilizing metamodel sparse grids, this method efficiently employs fewer parameters compared to other grid-based techniques, making it an attractive choice for numerically solving moderate- and higher-dimensional problems.

The conclusive step in this analysis involves seeking the optimal solution through the simplest screening method, which involves identifying the corresponding cell average temperature at a specific set of inlet parameters. The Ansys Design Exploration toolbox serves as the primary tool for conducting all aspects of the parametric analysis.

Air cooling was considered the most conventional method for battery cooling, encompassing two variations: free cooling and forced cooling. These systems were characterized by simplicity, cost-effectiveness, electrical safety, compactness, leak resistance, and low maintenance requirements. While natural cooling sufficed in certain conditions, it proved inadequate for high-temperature environments, larger battery modules, and increased charge-discharge cycles. To address these limitations, forced-air cooling was employed, often involving the installation of fans, designed air-flow channels, fin systems, and similar enhancements [15].

A 3S3P module was deliberately developed and utilized for the evaluation of various cooling configurations. Specifically, cylindrical lithium batteries were employed for the module's development. A comparison between aligned and staggered battery module arrangements revealed that the former demonstrated superior cooling effectiveness and temperature uniformity. Battery thermal management system was installed for cross pack [25]. For modules with straight and staggered setups, a transverse cell-to-cell gap of 34 mm is considered optimal [26]. This variance in gap spacing proved to significantly enhance the heat dissipation performance of the battery [27]. An assumed cell-to-wall spacing of 5 mm was considered. Additionally, it was noted that the width of the cooling channel had a discernible impact on the overall cooling performance [28]. Earlier research allowed the air flow through cell-to-cell gaps which makes the safety of module vulnerable [29]. An aluminum container was devised to prevent the ingress of air into the battery module. Interestingly, the supporting structure for the battery cells was observed to have minimal influence on cell temperature. The thermal hotspot was concentrated at the electrode rather than being uniformly distributed across the entire battery. This localized hotspot resulted in temperature variations that affected both the cell's lifespan and overall performance. Additionally, the insulation layer and busbar thicknesses were assumed to be 1 mm each. These components were arranged to form a 3S3P battery module. The material properties of each component were obtained from various sources, are presented on Table 1 below.

The design of air-cooling configurations was undertaken, taking into account battery module specifications such as size and capacity. Fig. 8 below depicts the geometries of various airflow channels that were under consideration. These kinds of configurations have already been done for rectangular or prismatic cells [31]. The primary objective was to monitor the variations in cell average temperature due to differences in air flow configurations, specifically under the influence of forced convection. This observation was conducted while maintaining a constant air inlet area irrespective of air contact time and outlet area.

To model this, 3S3P arrangement of standard 18,650 cells was constructed using CAD software, drawing from pertinent parameters found in literature. The CAD model was then transformed into Parasolid format for integration within Ansys Design Modeler, enabling subsequent meshing and comprehensive analysis in Ansys fluent.

The determination of heat generation involved the utilization of the experimental setup detailed in Section 2.1. Parameters essential for Bernardi's Equation were measured, enabling the calculation of heat generation for the lithium-ion cell INR8650 25S. Among the various parameters measured, including open circuit voltage, terminal voltage, resistor values, and current passing through the resistor, these measurements were instrumental in computing the internal resistance of the cell. The specific details regarding these parameters and the internal resistance values derived from these measurements are presented in Table 2
below.

The calculation of the overall heat generation rate utilizing Bernardi's equation is conducted based on the parameters obtained from the experimental setups for both Joule's heating and entropic heating. The outcomes derived from this computation are outlined and displayed in Table 4 below.

The negative sign observed in the entropic heat signifies that the system released heat during discharge, indicating decrement in randomness of system with respect to surrounding. The cumulative heat generated by individual cells was ultimately assessed to be 0.9028 Watts.

An experimental study was carried out using a unit cell to validate the efficacy of conjugate heat transfer simulations and to analyze response curves concerning the cooling of a battery module. The unit cell module was specifically designed and exposed to air cooling at different velocities. The experiment was conducted at an average rate of 0.85C.Also, the inlet temperature directly affects the maximum temperature [31]. Thus, overall ambient temperature was kept at 295 K.Cooling was implemented at discrete velocities ranging from 0.2 m/s, 1.3 m/s, 3.2 m/s, to 4.3 m/s by adjusting the voltage from the power source. During both the charging and discharging cycles, there was noticeable temperature variation over time. Graphical analysis was employed on the experimental data to comprehend the trend of temperature variation concerning the velocity for the unit cell module during air cooling in Fig. 9 below.

Fig. 9 illustrates elevation in the average cell temperature during natural convection, particularly noticeable at an airflow velocity of around 0.2 m/s. However, as the system shifts towards forced convection at velocities of 1.3 m/s, 3.2 m/s, and 4.3 m/s, a notable reduction in the average cell temperature becomes evident. The observed decrease in the average cell temperature as the airflow velocity increases can be ascribed to the simultaneous rise in convective heat transfer. This trend aligns with findings from a corroborating study [32], which supports and validates similar results. The data presented portrayed the transient behavior of the module's temperature during the cooling process. To draw steady-state comparisons with simulated values, the modal value was taken into consideration for each specific velocity, facilitating a more stable point of reference for comparison purposes.

The direct simulation for the unit cell module was conducted utilizing commercial software Ansys Fluent. During the processing stage, the air inlet velocity was specified as an input parameter. The conjugate heat transfer analysis was employed to analyze and assess the average temperature within the system. Varied air inlet velocities were applied while maintaining an ambient temperature of 295K.

For each cell, a heating generation of 0.9028 W was observed, and an average current of 2.13A was passed through the electrode, resulting in Joule's law of heating being observed within the system. The air inlet velocity was systematically altered four times to monitor the resulting cell average temperatures, which are presented in Table 5 above. At first place it was done with natural convection resulting cell average temperature of 309.28 K. However, with effect of forced air convection at flow velocity of 1.3 m/s,3.2 m/s and 4.3 m/s, cell average temperature for single cell module dropped to 301.27K, 299,76K and 299.46K respectively. It can be clearly indicated that there is an abrupt drop of 8K temperature with an increase in flow velocity to 1.3 m/s. This is because of convective heat transfer which increased heat transfer rate reducing the cell average temperature.

The parametric analysis was conducted in sequential stages, outlined as depicted in Fig. 7. Following the completion of the base case simulation employing conjugate heat transfer methodology, the subsequent step involved parametric analysis. The input design parameter incorporated air inlet velocity, whereas the primary output parameter considered was the average cell temperature. The air inlet velocity spans a range from 0.1 m/s to 5 m/s, limited by the capacity of the fan and set as an input in discrete increments of four values. The Design of Experiments (DOE) process incorporates Sparse Grid initialization. To begin this initialization, specific values of 0.1 m/s, 2.55 m/s, and 5 m/s are selected. These values serve as parameters to simulate the cell average temperature utilizing conjugate heat transfer analysis within Ansys Fluent.

Utilizing the experiment design outlined in Table 6 , a response curve was constructed through the application of the sparse grid method using refinement points. The associated Fig. 10 depicts the dataset of response curve analysis. Every data point within the figure represents the cell average temperature output derived from individual simulation processes, each aligned with its respective velocity parameter.

Using the datasets derived from the response curve, an optimization process was executed to obtain the temperatures corresponding to given velocities. This optimization was conducted employing a screening method. The outcomes of this optimization process are tabulated in Table 7 below for comparative analysis with the experimental values. Additionally, the table incorporates the calculated error percentages to assess the disparities between the optimized and experimental data.

The disparities observed between the experimental data and the response curve analysis could stem from simplifications made in assuming electrical and thermal material properties within the model. The approximate accuracy of the heat generation calculation, crucial for input in the conjugate heat transfer coupling of the battery model, might have contributed to the deviations observed in the response. Moreover, there could be additional forms of heat exchange occurring in the experiments that were not accounted for in the numerical analysis.

Notwithstanding these factors, it shows that the errors observed in the comparison remain below 3 % for all instances. This suggests that despite the identified limitations, the method of response curve analysis provides reasonably accurate results for analyzing the air cooling of battery modules under similar conditions.

The investigation into air cooling configurations aimed to identify the optimal method for effectively cooling a 3S3P battery module. This analysis was conducted through direct simulation and parametric analysis, employing the generation of response curves as a key methodological approach.

Direct simulation was employed to analyze the cell average temperature at a specific velocity point, set at 10 m/s, across various configurations. Ansys Fluent served as the analysis tool, facilitating the extraction of corresponding temperature contours. These contours are visually represented and detailed in Fig. 11 below.

Fig. 11 illustrates temperature contours representing the four specified configurations. The respective cell average temperatures for SISO, SIDO, U-shape, and Z-shape configurations are recorded as 307.68K, 307.60K, 308.81K, and 308.02K, respectively. Notably, the direct simulation indicates that the SIDO configuration exhibits a comparatively lower cell average temperature, signifying its effectiveness in the air-cooling process for the 3S3P module. This phenomenon can be attributed to the configuration with two outlets, which facilitates enhanced airflow, leading to improved thermal management. This arrangement ensures more uniform cooling across cells by enabling adequate and consistent air distribution. This observation aligns with the findings verified by the study cited in paper [15].

The methodology involved a parametric analysis employing the Ansys Design Exploration toolbox, focusing on diverse configurations of the air-cooling system's flow channel, including U-shaped, Z-shaped, SISO, and SIDO configurations. Velocity ranging from 0.1 m/s to 40 m/s were supplied to cool the battery module. Conversely, the output parameter of interest was the cell average temperature. The relationship between the cell average temperature and air velocity is illustrated as follows.

It is evident that the SIDO-shaped configuration consistently maintains a lower cell average temperature across the entire range of provided velocities. This observation also suggests that beyond a specific velocity threshold, the design configuration also has a negligible effect on the cell average temperature. The increase in cooling air velocity results in a reduction of the cell average temperature across all configuration types due to the amplified convective heat transfer. This trend is depicted in Fig. 12 , showcasing a consistent decrease in temperature from an air velocity of 0.1 m/s to 40 m/s.

Interestingly, beyond a flow velocity of 20 m/s, a stable cell average temperature is observed, indicating a point where further increases in air velocity yield minimal impact on temperature reduction. At higher flow velocities, it is noted that the heat transfer coefficient does not notably increase, thereby limiting the increment of convective heat transfer to higher levels, as supported by findings from Ref. [33]. The internal configuration and thermal pathways within the battery may not uniformly respond to increased airflow. Moreover, an increase in flow velocity tends to result in a thinner boundary layer, impeding the interaction between the fluid and the surface. This limitation subsequently reduces the heat transfer rate by constraining their mutual contact, as highlighted in Ref. [32].

The discrepancy observed between the values obtained from the curve response and direct simulation indicates the limitations in the response curve's capability to precisely approximate temperatures at specific points. This divergence can also be attributed to the utilization of a specific order of polynomial in curve fitting through sparse grid methodology, aiming to minimize errors.

This study offers significant insights into implementing effective air-cooling configurations for ingress-protected modules in real-world applications. The list of conclusions that are drawn from the work presented above are listed below.
•
Through an experiment conducted on a unit cell module, it was determined that heat generation occurred at a rate of 0.9028 W during average charging rate at 0.85C.
•
With increase in the velocity of air through forced convection, there was decrease in the cell average temperature for all types of the configuration from velocity range of 0.1 ms^-1to 40 ms^-1.
•
At a velocity of 10 ms^-1, direct simulation method revealed the following average cell temperatures for the configurations: SISO - 307.68K, SIDO - 307.60K, U-shape - 308.81K, and Z-shape - 308.02K. Notably, the SIDO configuration consistently maintains a lower average cell temperature compared to the other configurations.
•
The response curve analysis method also indicated that the SIDO configurations consistently maintain a lower average cell temperature across all specified velocity ranges compared to others.
•
The validation of the response curve method involved a unit cell module configuration, demonstrating a precise alignment between observed temperature from experimentation and those obtained through parametric analysis under similar conditions. This validation exhibits only 3 % relative error within the flow velocity range of 0.2–4.3 ms^-1 for unit cell module.

In addition, the utilization of the innovative response curve method for parametric analysis across a range of input values to observe cell average temperature suggests a potential reduction in analysis time, particularly for tasks demanding substantial computational resources. Thus, this approach could present a convenient and cost-effective battery thermal analysis method for the electric vehicle industry technology.

Daya Bandhu Ghimire: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Visualization, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Sanjeev Maharjan: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Software, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Saroj Neupane: Conceptualization, Data curation, Investigation. Abhishek Uprety: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
